title: Progress Review - January
text:
Contents

Review action points
Progress with literature review
Progress with AITL Visualization
Agreed Action points

title: Progress Review - January
text:
From the last meeting
Agreed action points:


On geovisualization platform:

Try to do the aggregation on the backend → combine the data (e.g. time series) through some statistical measure to multivariate layer, then send them to client side and process the visualization (glyph) based on the aggregated data
Don’t constraint too much on shape (H3), or libraries (Maplibre). Focus on what is actually needed for the visualization, then starts from there

On Literature review:

ensure that no previous paper discussing about literature review of state-of-the-art in multivariate geospatial visualization
this could lead to a specific STAR paper discussing about the topic

Paper preparation:

For EuroVIS, write up the STAR paper (P0), and start from there with P1, work in parallel with other ideas
Need some discussion with AITL to have sample of data which possess spatio-temporal characteristics (e.g. carbon intensity) on a MSOA level

title: Progress Review - January
text:
From the last meeting
Papers:
P0: A STAR paper, focusing on state-of-the-art, systematic review of multivariate geospatial data visualization
P1: A Design Space paper on multivariate geospatial data visualization, specifically on glyph visualization with guidelines from Aidan’s paper
P2: User evaluation of P1
P3: Domain problem paper on Covid Dataset
P3-like: Domain problem paper on Energy Dataset

title: Progress Review - January
text:
Progress with Literature review
On P0:

The VISpub dataset (Isenberg et.al., 2017)
Visualization of VISpub dataset by Isenberg, et.al., 2017b
Geospatial visualization review (Yoshizumi et.al., 2020)

title: Progress Review - January
text:
Progress with Literature review (P0)
The VISpub dataset (Isenberg et.al., 2017):

A curated database of papers published in VIS conferences
Last update: 2021. As of now, consist of 3504 papers published in vis conferences (VIS, INFOVIS, VAST, SCIVIS)

title: Progress Review - January

Conferences included:

Utilizing information extraction and manual adjustments

Some important takeaways. List of related keywords on geovisualization:

Yoshizumi, et.al. (2020)

title: Progress Review - January
text:
Progress with Literature review (P0)
Yoshizumi, et.al. (2020)

a need to define and limit the term ‘geovisualization’
The method used in the review was a systematic analysis of 585 papers.
Two reviewers examined each paper to identify where geospatial data was used and generated keywords and short descriptions for it.

title: Progress Review - January
text:
Progress with Literature review (P0)
Yoshizumi, et.al. (2020)


Papers are selected based on questions such as above, utilizing word filter
A separate team then synthesized this information, made final decisions on category selection, and identified 94 IEEE VIS papers from years 2017-2019 that had geospatial content.
“increasing trend towards blurring divisions between SciVis & InfoVis tracks”

title: Progress Review - January
text:
Progress with Literature Review (P0)
Ideas: 1. Augment the existing datasets with other set of data: * My own curated papers. This includes papers that are outside of VIS area (i.e., papers that are published in GIS conferences/journals) * CGF/Eurovis dataset, especially the STAR papers as published by Chen (2020) * Similar paper collections in GIS conferences (I can’t find any for now, but some position papers discuss about the intersection of GIS and visualization domain, e.g., Schiewe, 2013, and Christophe, 2020)

title: Progress Review - January
text:
Progress with Literature Review (P0)

Use visual analytics as a tool to extract visualization methods used for geospatial analysis
Some examples:

Observable Notebook by ‘@Fil’ (Data up to 2021)
Keyvis (Isenberg, 2017)
Observable Notebook by John Guerra (Data up to 2020)

title: Progress Review - January
text:
Progress with Literature Review (P0)
Ideas:

Build a ‘proper’ database and website consisting of the original Isenberg dataset and allows registered users to add more data to the database
Use LLMs to extract information during literature review/visual analytics process
A rough example
A literate programming approach for literature review: A WIP

title: Progress Review - January
text:
Progress with Literature Review (P1)

Lots of papers discussing about Glyph (Borgo, et.al., 2012; Ward, 2008; Fuchs, 2017, etc..)
Highly varied in terms of method and visualization design
could start from the Isenberg Vispub dataset for review

title: Progress Review - January
text:
Progress with geovisualization platform
Had a meeting with Lily, discussing about AITL’s visualization needs. Some key takeaways:
I will be involved in some client meetings to better understand their needs
The three main actors (’users’):

Local Authorities
DNOs
Consultants

title: Progress Review - January
text:
Progress with geovisualization platform
Two problems that AITL tries to tackle regarding data:

How to model the network needs in a lower granularity (e.g. Building level, by DNOs)
How to model decision making in a higher level granularity (local authorities)

title: Progress Review - January
text:
Progress with geovisualization platform

AITL has been developing a Dashboard and Data stories platform
Some GIS problems which requires visualization design decision

title: Progress Review - January
text:
Action points

Write up the P0 paper based on the ideas as mentioned
Start a literature review on P1
Working on the platform as discussed with AITL

title: Presentations
text:
The Challenge: A ‘Wicked Problem’
Developing effective and equitable decarbonisation plans is a complex balancing act for UK local authorities.


They must balance:

Technical factors (Carbon reduction)
Economic factors (Costs, budgets)
Social factors (Fuel poverty, equity)


Resulting in a ‘wicked problem’:

Conflicting stakeholder priorities.
Massive, multivariate datasets.
No single ‘optimal’ solution.

title: Presentations
text:
The Gap in Existing Tools


Traditional GIS


The ‘map layer metaphor’ increases cognitive load by forcing users to mentally integrate fragmented information.


‘Black Box’ Optimisation


Monolithic optimisation models hide the trade-offs inherent in the planning process.



Traditional GIS forces users to flip between layers; black-box optimisation hides trade-offs and the “why” behind results.

title: Presentations
text:
Why Simulation, Not Just Optimisation?
For a ‘wicked problem’, the goal is not a single optimal answer, but to explore a range of plausible and defensible futures.


Optimisation

Asks: “What is the best solution?”
Finds a single, mathematically optimal result.
Treats qualitative goals as rigid constraints.
Hides inherent trade-offs inside the model.


Simulation (Our Approach)

Asks: “If we do X, what happens?”
Enables multiple what-if scenarios.
Augments human judgment for complex goals.
Makes trade-offs explicit and visible.



We ask a different question: “If we make this decision, what happens?” It augments expert judgment and makes trade-offs explicit.

title: Presentations
text:
Our Contribution: A Human-in-the-Loop Paradigm
We built a visual analytics system on three core contributions to enable this exploratory process.

Modular, Component-Based Planning
Decomposes complex strategy-building into manageable, interpretable parts.
Multi-Scale, Glyph-Based Visualisation
Represents multivariate and temporal data uniformly across geographic scales.
An Integrated, Iterative Workflow
Tightly couples data exploration, simulation, and outcome analysis for real-time feedback.

title: Presentations
text:
The Decarbonisation Planner


The main interface integrates intervention authoring, data tables, a scenario timeline, and a glyph-map for outcome analysis.

title: Presentations
text:
A Workflow for Policy Exploration
Our system supports an iterative, four-step process (T1-T4):

Identify & Characterise Cohorts (T1)
Use linked map and table to filter and select groups of buildings.
Author Scenarios via Composition (T2)
Define and layer modular plan components on the timeline.
Compare Spatio-Temporal Outcomes (T3)
Instantly simulate impacts and visualise them on the glyph-map.
Explore Alternatives through Iteration (T4)
Adjust parameters, re-order components, and immediately see the results.

title: Presentations
text:
Visualising Complex Trade-offs with Glyphs


Left: radial glyph for multi-attribute potential. Right: mirrored streamgraph showing Carbon Saved (green) vs. Cost (red) over time.

title: Presentations
text:
Managing Scale & Analytical Clarity


Data is represented consistently across scales. Geography can be ‘squarified’ to give areas equal visual prominence.

title: Presentations
text:
Merits & Limitations


Merits

Transparent & interpretable
Reduces cognitive load
Fosters iteration & dialogue


Limitations

Need for formal evaluation
Model simplification
Scalability

title: Presentations
text:
Conclusion & Future Work
We reframed decarbonisation planning from a rigid, monolithic process into a transparent, flexible, and iterative dialogue.
Future work:

Conduct contextual user studies
Enhance simulation fidelity
Develop side-by-side scenario comparison

title: Presentations
text:
Thank You
D. Laksono, R. Jianu, & A. Slingsby
Live Demo: https://decarb-vis.netlify.app
Source Code: https://github.com/danylaksono/decarbonisation-glyphmap-planner

title: Presentations
text:
Please keep the issue tracker limited to bug reports, feature requests and pull requests.


If you have personal support or setup questions the best place to ask those are StackOverflow.



When reporting a bug make sure to include information about which browser and operating system you are on as well as the necessary steps to reproduce the issue. If possible please include a link to a sample presentation where the bug can be tested.




Should follow the coding style of the file you work in, most importantly:

Tabs to indent
Single-quoted strings

Should be made towards the dev branch
Should be submitted from a feature/topic branch (not your master)




Please do not submit plugins as pull requests. They should be maintained in their own separate repository. More information here: https://github.com/hakimel/reveal.js/wiki/Plugin-Guidelines
